message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  We previously reported that the
                  <jats:italic>ApoE</jats:italic>
                  e4e4 genotype was associated with COVID-19 test positivity (OR=2.31, 95% CI: 1.65 to 3.24, p=1.19×10
                  <jats:sup>−6</jats:sup>
                  ) in the UK Biobank (UKB) cohort, during the epidemic peak in England, from March 16 to April 26, 2020. With more COVID-19 test results (March 16 to May 31, 2020) and mortality data (to April 26, 2020) linked to UKB, we re-evaluated the
                  <jats:italic>ApoE</jats:italic>
                  e4 allele association with COVID-19 test positivity, and with all-cause mortality following test-confirmed COVID-19. Logistic regression models compared
                  <jats:italic>ApoE</jats:italic>
                  e4e4 participants (or e3e4s) to e3e3s with adjustment for sex; age on April 26th or age at death; baseline UKB assessment center in England (accounting for geographical differences in viral exposures); genotyping array type; and the top five genetic principal components (accounting for possible population admixture).
                  <jats:italic>ApoE</jats:italic>
                  e4e4 genotype was associated with increased risks of test positivity (OR=2.24, 95% CI: 1.72 to 2.93, p=3.24×10
                  <jats:sup>−9</jats:sup>
                  ) and of mortality with test-confirmed COVID-19 (OR=4.29, 95% CI: 2.38 to 7.72, p=1.22×10
                  <jats:sup>−6</jats:sup>
                  ), compared to e3e3s. Independent replications are needed to confirm our findings and mechanistic work is needed to understand how
                  <jats:italic>ApoE</jats:italic>
                  e4e4 results in the marked increase in vulnerability, especially for COVID-19 mortality. These findings also demonstrate that risks for COVID-19 mortality are not simply related to advanced chronological age or the comorbidities commonly seen in aging.
                </jats:p>
title: ApoE e4e4 genotype and mortality with COVID-19 in UK Biobank

reference.unstructured: Kuo C-L , Pilling LC , Atkins JL , Masoli JAH , Delgado J , Kuchel GA , Melzer D. APOE e4 genotype predicts severe COVID-19 in the UK Biobank community cohort. Journals Gerontol Ser A [Internet]. 2020;. Available from: https://doi.org/10.1093/gerona/glaa131
